segments.s340-g41: به introduces a locative sense here ("at/in/for"). هر برزنی is a noun phrase meaning "every barzan/village square" (هر = "each/every" before the noun). جشنگاهی is a compound noun (جشن + گاه) meaning "a place of celebration," and سده postmodifies it to specify which festival ("the Sadeh festival"); Persian often places such specifying nouns after the head noun in poetic diction.
segments.s340-g42: همه‌گرد functions adverbially here (literally "all‑around/ubiquitous"), modifying the following phrase. بر is the preposition meaning "on/upon/around" and combines with گردش ("circuit/turn") to mean "around the circuit." آتشکده is the object of گردش ("the circuit of the fire‑temple"); note the compact, archaic/poetic word order without an explicit ezāfe linking word.
segments.s340-g43: یکی آذری is a subject phrase meaning "a certain Azeri" (یکی = "a certain/someone" before the nationality word). ساخت is the past tense of ساختن ("built/made"). برزین به نام is an appositive/name phrase: به نام = "by the name of / called," so the name (برزین) follows the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s340-g44: که is a relative pronoun meaning "who" linking to the previous noun (the Azeri). با + name(s) indicates accompaniment or association ("with Farrokhi"); بود is the past copula "was." The second با repeats the sense "with," and the coordinated برز و کام lists two items joined by و; in this context they function as companions/names (poetic coordination), so the clause reads "who was with Farrokhi and with Barz and Kam."
segments.s340-g9: 'چو' here is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'; it introduces a subordinate clause that ends with the past-tense transitive verb 'آفرید' ('he created'). The list 'دریا و کوه و زمین' are direct objects of آفرید, joined by 'و' in normal coordinating order.
segments.s340-g10: The phrase places the adjective 'بلند' before the noun 'آسمان' ('the lofty sky'). 'از' means 'from/out of' and links this phrase to the previous clause (i.e. 'above/from it'); 'برش' here denotes 'its surface/side' (poetic use) and 'برکشید' is a past transitive verb 'he raised' with implied subject 'he' from the previous clause. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but functionally: he raised the lofty sky from/over it.
segments.s340-g11: 'یکی' is the numeral 'one' introducing the first of two items; 'تیز گردان' is an adjectival/participial phrase 'sharp/fast-turning' (i.e. 'made it spin swiftly'). The coordinating 'و' links to 'دیگر بجای' — 'the other in its place' — where 'بجای' means 'in its place / stationary'. The compact order is poetic: item + modifier rather than a full clause with an explicit verb for both items.
segments.s340-g12: This is a short clause with the verb 'ندادش' ('did not give it'): the negative-prefixed past 'نداد' plus enclitic object 'ش' ('it/him'). 'به جنبش' ('motion') is the object introduced by the preposition 'به' ('to/with'); 'نگارنده' (here 'the Ordainer/Creator') is the agent and follows the verb for poetic word order; 'پای' ('foot/base') names what was not given motion. Overall: 'the Ordainer did not give motion to its base.'
segments.s340-g13: 'چو' here begins a simile/temporal clause 'as/when'; 'موی از بر گوی' is a fixed simile 'a hair on the orb' (موی = 'hair' + از بر گوی = 'on the surface of the sphere'). 'و ما در میان' continues the thought: 'and we in the midst' — 'در میان' = 'in the middle'. The clause omits an explicit verb (typical poetic ellipsis) and sets the scene for what follows.
segments.s340-g14: This is a prepositional phrase headed by 'به' meaning 'with/subject to': 'به رنج تن' = 'with the toil/suffering of the body'. The following nouns 'آز' (greed), 'سود' (profit) and 'زیان' (loss) are coordinated with 'و', listing the various burdens or conditions the speaker says we endure.
segments.s340-g15: This is a compound nominal phrase with no overt copula: 'تو شادان دل' ('you joyful‑hearted') puts the pronoun تو first followed by the adjective phrase شادان دل (شادان modifying دل), a poetic adjective meaning 'happy/joyful of heart'. The conjunction و links this phrase to the next noun phrase مرگ چنگال تیز, where the head noun مرگ ('death') is followed by the noun چنگال ('claw') plus the adjective تیز ('sharp'); in Persian modifiers commonly follow the noun, so مرگ چنگال تیز means 'death (with) sharp claws.'
segments.s340-g16: نشسته is a past‑participle/adjectival verb meaning 'seated' or 'sitting' used predicatively; here it attaches back to مرگ in the previous segment ('death sits'). چو is the comparative 'like/as', introducing a simile. شیر is the noun 'lion', and the two words ژیان پرستیز form a post‑nominal descriptive unit: ژیان 'life' + پرستیز (an older noun/adjective 'prestige, renownedness') — together giving the sense 'proud/renowned in life' or 'proud in its life', describing the lion in the simile.
segments.s340-g25: بسازید is a 2nd-person plural imperative (or hortative) of ساختن ‘to make/establish’ commanding the audience to ‘set right’ or ‘act (make) [something]’. و simply coordinates the two imperatives. از here means “because of / on account of,” followed by داد ‘justice’ (noun). باشید is the 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive/imperative form of بودن used as a verbal command ‘be’, and شاد is an adjective ‘happy/joyful’ predicated of the addressees. Together: “Make/establish, and because of justice be glad.”
segments.s340-g26: تن آسان is a verbless/adjectival phrase meaning ‘(let) the body be at ease’ (تن = body; آسان = easy/at ease). The coordinating و links this clause to the previous one. از here means ‘concerning / of’ (introducing the object of remembrance), کین is ‘hatred/vengeance’, and مگیرید is the 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive/negative-imperative form ‘do not take / do not hold’ (the negative sense is signaled by مـ; in older/poetic Persian this is the form used for prohibitions). یاد is the noun ‘remembrance / memory’ governed by مگیرید: ‘do not take/keep remembrance (of hatred).’
segments.s340-g27: مهان is a plural noun ‘guests / esteemed visitors’ (here used for the assembled people or nobles). جهان‌آفرین is an epithet ‘world‑creator’ placed after مهان as the name/epithet being applied. خواندند is the simple past 3rd-person plural ‘they called/named’ (here ‘they addressed/called [him]’). In this poetic order the object of naming (him) is supplied in the next clause (ورا).
segments.s340-g28: ورا is an enclitic/oblique pronoun meaning ‘to him’ (i.e., the person addressed). شهریار is the noun ‘king / sovereign’, زمین is ‘earth / land’, and خواندند repeats the past-tense ‘they called’ (they named him ‘king of the land’). Word order here places the indirect object/target (ورا) before the predicate phrase formed by شهریار زمین خواندند.
segments.s340-g29: گرانمایه is an epithet/adjective ‘precious / high‑valued’ placed before the proper name لهراسپ (Lohrasp), a common poetic order in Persian. آرام is a predicative adjective ‘calm/at rest’, and یافت is the simple past 3rd-person singular ‘he found/obtained’. The whole clause says that Lohrasp (described as precious) ‘found rest/peace’.
segments.s340-g30: خرد مایه is a collocation in which خرد ‘wisdom’ is qualified by مایه ‘source / fund’, i.e. ‘a source of wisdom’. و coordinates the next nominal phrase. کام پدرام is a genitive construction ‘the pleasure/satisfaction (کام) of Pedram’ (پدرام is a personal name); یافت again is past ‘he found/obtained’. The subject (‘he’, Lohrasp) is understood across both coordinated predicates.
segments.s340-g31: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial ‘from that after’ = ‘after that / thereafter’ (از = from, آن = that, پس = after/then). فرستاد is the simple past 3rd-person singular ‘he sent’. کسها (plural) means ‘people / persons’ (here the ones sent). به introduces the destination (‘to’) and روم is ‘Rome / Byzantium’. The clause follows the common Persian order placing the verb before its object and complements in narrative style.
segments.s340-g32: This segment lists destinations using repeated به ‘to’ before each name: به هند (to India) و به چین (and to China) و به آباد بوم (and to prosperous/inhabited lands). آباد modifies بوم (land/homeland), so آباد بوم means ‘prosperous land / flourishing region’. The repetition of به is a stylistic/poetic coordinate structure enumerating the places sent to.
segments.s340-g33: ز هر مرز هرکس که دانا بدند is a compact relative clause introduced by ز (poetic 'from'). هر مرز 'from every border' is a genitive/locative phrase; هرکس که دانا (literally 'whoever that wise') is a relative clause with که as the relativizer. دانا is an adjective placed after the relativized noun phrase (common in classical Persian). بدند is an archaic past/plural form ('they were' / 'they became') serving as the finite verb of the clause.
segments.s340-g34: به پیمانش اندر توانا بدند continues the main predication: اندر + adjective + verb marks a state 'in (regard to) his covenant, (they) were capable/strong'. به پیمانش 'regarding his pledge' or 'in his pact' is a prepositional phrase; اندر is an older-style locative preposition ('in, within') emphasizing the domain where توانـا ('capable/strong') holds. بدند again is the archaic past/plural verb agreeing with the implied plural or generic people.
segments.s340-g35: ز هر کشوری بر گرفتند راه: ز هر کشوری 'from every country' is the source phrase; بر + گرفتند here functions as a compound (برگرفتند) meaning 'took up' or 'set out on' — in classical usage بر can precede the verb as a separable preverb or preposition-like element. راه is the object/complement 'the road' (the thing taken up). Word order places the particle/preverb before the verb, then the direct object.
segments.s340-g36: برفتند پویان به نزدیک شاه: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/they departed' (classical past). پویان is a participial/adverbial form 'moving/active/seekers' modifying the verb (here adverbially: 'traveling' or 'in haste'). به نزدیک شاه is a directional phrase 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه). The verb precedes the adverbial phrase, typical in older Persian.
segments.s340-g37: ز دانش چشیدند هر شور و تلخ: ز دانش 'from knowledge/learning' gives the source; چشیدند is the past plural of 'to taste' used figuratively as 'experienced'. هر modifies the coordinated nouns شور و تلخ (literally 'every passion and bitterness' — 'every joy and sorrow'). The adjective تلخ follows as the second coordinate; the verb governs the whole coordinated object.
segments.s340-g38: ببودند با کام چندی به بلخ: ببودند is an archaic/past plural copula-like form 'they were' (or 'they had been'); با کام 'with satisfaction/desire' is an instrumental/associative phrase; چندی here means 'for a while' (a temporal adverb); به بلخ marks the location 'in/at Balkh'. Together they state a temporary state experienced in Balkh. Word order places the temporal adverb (چندی) after the noun phrase it modifies.
segments.s340-g39: یکی شارسانی برآورد شاه: یکی 'a certain / one' introduces the person (indefinite). شارسانی is a proper/name or title; classical Persian often places the person before the verb. برآورد is an older past form meaning 'brought forward / produced / set forth' (here transitive, with شاه as the actor). شاه follows as the subject of the clause (verb–subject order is common in Ferdowsi).
segments.s340-g40: پر از برزن و کوی و بازارگاه: پر از is the idiom 'full of' (پر followed by از + noun). The three coordinated nouns برزن, کوی, بازارگاه ('fields/plazas', 'alleys/quarters', 'marketplaces') specify what is filled. This segment is a nominal phrase describing the extent or character of the person/place introduced in the previous clause.
segments.s340-g17: ز here marks cause ('because of'); the phrase آز و فزونی is a coordinated noun phrase ('greed and excess'). به یکسو is a directional phrase ('toward one side' / 'one-sided'), and شویم is a first‑person plural present/subjunctive form of شدن placed at the end (typical Persian verb‑final order) meaning 'we become' or 'we turn'.
segments.s340-g18: به marks the cause/means here ('by/through'); نادانی خویش is a noun phrase where خوی + the clitic ش (his/her/its) functions reflexively ('one's own disposition/folly'); خستو is an adjective 'weary/worn out' and شویم again is the verb 'we become' closing the clause. The possessive clitic is written separately in the segmentation but functions as part of خویش.
segments.s340-g19: ازین is the preposition از + demonstrative (here 'from this'); تاج شاهی is a genitival phrase 'the crown of kingship' (شاهی specifying تاج), و links an additional noun تخت, and بلند is a postpositive adjective modifying تخت ('lofty'/'high'). Word order is noun + modifier, and the whole phrase is the source from which the speaker speaks.
segments.s340-g20: نجوییم is jussive/negative construction from جستن with the negative prefix ن‑ (here functioning with جز to mean 'we seek nothing except'); جز introduces the limited list of what is sought. داد، آرام، پند are coordinate objects separated by و. The verb remains at the end of the clause (typical Persian order).
segments.s340-g21: مگر introduces an exception/condition here ('unless'/'except that'). بهره‌مان is بهره with the possessive suffix -مان ('our share'). زین سرای سپنج is a prepositional/nominal phrase indicating the source/lot ('from this house/dwelling' with سپنج as a descriptive noun); the phrase functions as the conditional complement of مگر.
segments.s340-g22: نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive verb meaning 'may ... not come / does not come'; همی is an adverbial intensifier ('ever/always' or 'at all'); the coordinated nouns کین و نفرین و رنج are the unwanted things ('hatred, curse, and suffering') that the speaker wishes not to befall them. Typical Persian places the verb before its coordinated objects when negated by نیاید.
segments.s340-g23: من is the emphatic subject 'I'. از here is partitive/source ('from' or 'by means of') introducing the material being increased (پند کیخسرو = 'Kay Khosrow's advice'). افزون کنم is a verb phrase with افزون ('more/added') + کنم (present/subjunctive/optative of کردن) meaning 'I will add' or 'I will increase'. Verb comes at clause end.
segments.s340-g24: ز marks origin ('from/out of') and دل is the noun 'heart' (the locus from which something is removed). کینه و آز are the coordinated objects ('hatred and greed'). بیرون کنَم is the compound of بیرون (out) + کنَم (I do/make) meaning 'I will drive out / I will put out' — a causative/resultative structure with the verb at the end.
segments.s340-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” لهراسپ is the proper name (subject). بنشست is the past tense (3rd sing.) of نشستن with the prefixed بـ common in classical/poetic Persian, meaning “sat down / took his seat.” بر تخت is a prepositional phrase “upon the throne.” داد here is a nominal/elliptical use meaning “justice” (understood as ‘he gave/dispensed justice’), a compact epic style where the verbal idea is implied.
segments.s340-g2: به is the preposition “to/onto.” شاهنشهی qualifies تاج as ‘of kingship/imperial,’ an attributive form. تاج is “crown.” بر سر is the standard locative phrase “on the head.” نهاد is the past tense (3rd sing.) of نهادن (“placed / put”), so the clause means ‘(he) placed the imperial crown upon his head.’
segments.s340-g3: جهان‌آفرین is a compound meaning “the world‑creator / Creator.” را is the definite object marker, marking جهان‌آفرین as the direct object. ستایش is the noun “praise,” and گرفت (past of گرفتن) is used idiomatically with a noun to mean “took up / offered / began” (i.e. ‘he offered praise’). Word order follows classical Persian: object + verb with را marking the object.
segments.s340-g4: نیایش is “prayer.” ورا is the enclitic pronoun variant of به or او meaning “to him” (i.e. ‘prayer to Him’). در + فزایش here expresses purpose/result: “for increase” (praying that there be increase). گرفت again is the past of گرفتن meaning “took up/offered” (he offered a prayer for increase). The phrase is a compact way to express ‘he prayed to Him for increase.’
segments.s340-g5: چنین گفت is a formulaic “thus he said” (چنین = “thus/so”; گفت is the past form ‘said’ — note the provided senses list did not include the verbal sense ‘said’). کز is the subordinating particle ‘that / because,’ introducing the reason clause. داور is ‘the Judge’ (here the divine Judge). داد is the noun ‘justice’ used adjectivally/elliptically to characterize the Judge (“(possessor of) justice”), and پاک is the adjective “pure.” The clause is compact (no copula) as is common in epic diction.
segments.s340-g6: پر امید باشید is an exhortation: پر (here “full (of)”) + امید (“hope”), with باشید as the 2nd‑person‑plural present/subjunctive used for polite imperative (‘be’ / ‘remain’). و links to the following phrase. با ترس و باک literally reads “with fear and with dread”; grammatically با is the preposition “with” repeated for emphasis and ترس / باک are coordinated nouns. The line thus counsels both hope and (reverent) fear simultaneously — a common theological pairing.
segments.s340-g7: نگارنده is an agentive noun here meaning “writer/ordainer/author” (one who composes or ordains). چرخ گردنده is a compound: چرخ (“wheel”/heaven) qualified by گردنده (“turning/rotating”), together “the revolving wheel” (a poetic image for the heavens/cosmos). اوست is the copula (او = he; ست = is), forming a nominal sentence ‘He is the ordainer of the revolving wheel.’
segments.s340-g8: فراینده is an adjective/participle meaning “advancing / proceeding / moving” modifying فره (“farr,” i.e. divine glory or royal splendor). بنده اوست is a nominal clause “is his servant” (بنده = servant; اوست = ‘he/it is’), so the whole phrase reads ‘the advancing divine glory is his servant.’ Word order places the modifying participle before the noun, then the predicate ‘is his servant.’

sentences.s340-s21: In every village square there was a place for celebrating Sadeh — all around the circuit of the fire‑temple.
sentences.s340-s22: A certain Azeri built it, by the name Barzin; he was with Farrokhi and with Barz and Kam.
sentences.s340-s5: When he created the sea, the mountains, and the earth, he raised the lofty heaven above them.
sentences.s340-s6: He made one spin swiftly and left another in its place; the Ordainer did not give motion to its base.
sentences.s340-s7: As (if) a hair on the sphere, and we in the middle, (we are) subject to bodily toil, greed, profit and loss.
sentences.s340-s8: You are joyful‑hearted, and Death, sharp‑clawed, sits like a lion proud in life.
sentences.s340-s13: Be just and be glad; keep your body at ease, and do not bring to mind hatred.
sentences.s340-s14: They hailed him ‘World‑Creator’, and they called him sovereign of the earth.
sentences.s340-s15: The precious Lohrasp found rest; he gained a source of wisdom and obtained Pedram’s satisfaction.
sentences.s340-s16: After that he sent men to Rome, to India, to China, and to prosperous lands.
sentences.s340-s17: From every border, whoever was wise proved himself strong in upholding his pledge.
sentences.s340-s18: From every country they set out upon the road and went, traveling toward the king.
sentences.s340-s19: From learning they tasted every joy and bitterness, and for a while they had satisfaction in Balkh.
sentences.s340-s20: The king brought forward a certain Sharsan, filling the fields, quarters, and marketplaces.
sentences.s340-s9: Because of greed and excess we turn to one side; from our own ignorance we become weary.
sentences.s340-s10: From this royal crown and lofty throne we seek nothing except justice, calm, and counsel.
sentences.s340-s11: Unless our share from this dwelling be but a sponge, may hatred, curse, and suffering not come to us.
sentences.s340-s12: I will add to Kay Khosrow's counsel; I will drive hatred and greed out of my heart.
sentences.s340-s1: When Lohrasp took his seat on the throne, he administered justice and set the imperial crown upon his head.
sentences.s340-s2: He offered praise to the Creator; he prayed to Him for increase.
sentences.s340-s3: Thus he said: because the Judge is just and pure, be full of hope and with fear and dread.
sentences.s340-s4: He is the ordainer of the revolving wheel; the advancing divine glory is his servant.
